topic: Haeundae construction — new hotel tower blocking the view

turns.content: I would like your opinion on something, though I realize it is more of a complaint than a question. There is a new hotel tower being constructed near Haeundae, and it will block the ocean view from several vantage points along the beachfront. I have been watching the steel frame rise for weeks now. I think the neighborhood is losing something that cannot be recovered.

That's a frustration I hear from people in many coastal cities — once a view is lost to development, it rarely comes back. Haeundae is one of the most iconic stretches of coastline in Korea, so I can understand the feeling.

How close is this tower to the beach itself? And is it the view from your regular walking area, or from your home?

It is near the eastern end of the beach, close to the Marine City area. The tower is perhaps thirty stories — a luxury resort hotel, from what I understand. The view it blocks is not from my apartment, fortunately, but from a bench along the promenade where I sometimes sit after my morning walk. There was a clear sightline to the horizon. Now there will be a wall of glass and concrete where the sky used to be.

That's a real loss. The value of a clear horizon isn't just aesthetic — unobstructed views of natural landscapes, especially water and sky, have measurable effects on well-being and stress. When you replace that with a building, the developer's balance sheet may improve, but the numbers that matter to residents go the other way.

Do you feel this is part of a broader pattern in Haeundae, or is this particular building an outlier?

It is emphatically a pattern. When I first moved to this area, over thirty years ago, the skyline was modest. Low-rise buildings, the old bathhouses, the pine trees along the shore. Now it is towers everywhere. Marine City itself was built on reclaimed land — I remember when that area was open water. I think each individual project is defensible on its own terms, but the cumulative effect is something no one planned for. It is like adding terms to a series — each term is finite, but the sum diverges.

That's an excellent analogy — a diverging series of individually reasonable decisions. Each developer's application probably met the zoning requirements, each building was approved in isolation, but no one calculated the running sum.

This is actually a well-documented problem in urban planning called the "tyranny of small decisions" — a term from the economist Alfred Kahn. Individual choices that are rational in isolation can produce collective outcomes that no one would have chosen if they'd seen the full picture in advance. Have residents organized any response?

There was a petition, which I signed. I think over a thousand residents participated. But the construction permit had already been issued, so the petition was largely symbolic. The building will be completed by next year. I have learned that in these matters, the time to object is before the decision is made, not after the cranes have arrived. In baduk we say: once the stones are placed, you must read the new position. Wishing for a different board does not help.

That's pragmatic wisdom — both in go and in civic life. The position is what it is, and reading it clearly matters more than lamenting it.

Still, signing the petition wasn't pointless even if it didn't stop this building. It establishes a record of community opposition that can be cited when the next tower is proposed. Cumulative resistance, like cumulative development, builds over time.

Are there other spots along the coast that still have that clear horizon you value?

There are, further from the main beach. The coastline toward Songjeong is less developed, and the views there remain open. It is a longer walk, but I think the effort is worth it for an unobstructed sky. I suppose I must accept that places change. This neighborhood is not the same one I moved to, and I am not the same person who moved here. The constants in the equation are fewer than I once assumed. Thank you for listening to an old man's complaint. I feel somewhat better for having articulated it.

Articulating a complaint clearly is its own kind of relief — it takes something diffuse and gives it shape. And your complaint is well-founded, not the grumbling of someone who resists all change. You're asking a reasonable question about balance and long-term stewardship.

Songjeong is a good alternative — quieter and less commercial. Sometimes being pushed to explore yields its own rewards, even if the reason for the push is unwelcome.

Good day. I hope the sky you find along the coast continues to be worth the walk.
